segments.s332-g17: ببخشیدشان = بخشیدن (here used as a past action) + enclitic شان 'them'; the verb (ببخشید) takes the clitic object شان attached to it, so the clause reads 'he bestowed/gave them'. The following nouns خلعت و سیم و زر form a coordinated direct-object list ('robes and silver and gold'), joined by و meaning 'and'. Note the verb precedes its objects, a common poetic word order.
segments.s332-g18: یکی جام مر — یکی (the numeral 'one') modifies جام ('cup'), and مر is the old/dialectal dative pronoun 'to me' (i.e. 'one cup to me'). هر یکی را گهر — هر یکی ('each one') is fronted and marked by the object marker را before the noun گهر ('jewel'), giving the distributive sense 'a jewel for each one.' The word order places the beneficiary 'مر' after the noun and uses را to mark the distributed recipients.
segments.s332-g1: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning ‘he commanded/said’ and introduces a subordinate clause. تا here functions as the classical complementizer 'that/so that' (not temporal 'until'), marking the ordered action. رفت پیشش دبیر follows Persian word order where the verb (رفت) comes before its nominal subject (دبیر) and the locative (پیشش = ‘to his presence’) — together = ‘that a scribe go to him’.
segments.s332-g2: بیاورد is a third‑person verb (classical/subjunctive/jussive sense) expressing the intended action 'bring'. The following series قرطاس و مشک و عبیر are direct objects listed with و as simple coordinating 'and' — ‘paper, musk, and (a) fragrance’.
segments.s332-g3: نبشتند is the past plural 'they wrote'. عهدی is the indefinite object 'a covenant'. The preposition ز ('from' in poetic register) attaches to the following noun phrase شاه زمین = 'king of the land' (classical poetry often omits the ezafe), so نبشتند عهدی ز شاه زمین = 'they wrote a covenant from the king of the land.'
segments.s332-g4: This is a string of epithets in apposition to the preceding noun: سرافراز qualifies the king ('triumphant/honored'), کیخسرو is the proper name, and پاک‌دین is a descriptive epithet 'pure of religion' or 'pious'. Classical Persian often places multiple modifiers around a name without a copula.
segments.s332-g5: ز بهر is a classical phrase meaning 'for/on account of' or 'concerning'. سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a general). گو here functions like 'called' or a name‑forming element, linking to پیلتن to yield the personal name/title 'Go‑Piltan' (common in epic style to place the name after the title).
segments.s332-g6: ستوده is a passive/adjectival perfect 'praised'. ستوده به مردی uses به + abstract noun مردی ('manliness') to indicate the quality for which he is praised ('praised for his manliness'). The following به هر انجمن is a locative/setting phrase 'at every assembly' (classical Persian permits به for locations), so the whole segment says he is praised for manliness in every gathering.
segments.s332-g7: که begins a clause meaning 'that' or 'so that' and here introduces a wish/result clause. او is the pronoun 'he'. باشد is the classical third‑person subjunctive/jussive of 'to be' (rendering a hoped‑for or potential state: 'may he be'). اندر جهان sets the location 'in the world', and پیشرو is a predicate noun 'foremost/leading'.
segments.s332-g8: A coordinated list of predicate nouns (epithets) linked by و: جهاندار 'world‑holder/sovereign', بیدار 'awake/alert', سالار 'commander/chief', and گو used adjectivally 'speaking' (i.e. eloquent). Together they complete the predicate begun by باشد, describing the qualities he should have.
segments.s332-g9: This is an archaic word order meaning 'the realm belonged to him.' هم (fronted) = 'also/too'; او را بود is a past-copular construction where را marks the person who possesses (object/topic marking after the pronoun) and بود is the past copula 'was/held.' کشور نیمروز is the noun phrase 'the country/realm of Nimruz' (Nimruz as region).
segments.s332-g10: A sequence of titles/epithets stacked as a single nominal phrase. سپهدار is the head noun 'commander (spahbed)'; پیروز is an epithet 'victorious' modifying it; لشکر فروز is a second nominal phrase 'the glorious army' (فروز = 'glorious/bright'), yielding the sense 'the victorious commander of the glorious host.'
segments.s332-g11: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/established.' بر عهد is a prepositional phrase 'upon/under an oath/pledge.' A second preposition بر introduces مهر زر 'a (gold) seal' — together 'they set (it) on oath, under a gold seal.'
segments.s332-g12: بر آیین = 'according to/on the custom/rite' (بر = 'on/according to', آیین = 'custom, practice'). کیخسرو دادگر is the name plus epithet 'Kay Khosrow the Just' (دادگر used as an honorific). The whole phrase gives the law/custom under which the previous action was done.
segments.s332-g13: بدو is the dative/indirect-object pronoun 'to him/unto him.' داد is the past transitive 'gave' with منشور as its direct object 'a charter.' و کرد آفرین is a light-verb construction where کرد 'made/did' combines with the noun آفرین 'praise/acclamation' to mean 'and he praised/commended (him).' Order is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s332-g14: که introduces a subordinate clause (here a wishing/formulaic clause). آباد بادا is an optative formula: آباد = 'prosperous,' بادا = 'may it be' (archaic optative). به marks the beneficiary 'for/to Rostam,' and زمین is the thing wished well 'the land.' Word order places the adjective before the optative verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s332-g15: مهانی که begins a relative clause 'guests who...'. با زال سام = 'with Zal and Sam.' سوار here is postpositive and can be read as 'mounted' (an adjective) or 'horsemen' depending on context; syntactically it links as a description of those accompanying Zal and Sam.
segments.s332-g16: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/they went.' با زیجها is an accompaniment phrase 'with the zijs' (زیجها is plural; literal sense 'astronomical tables' per the headword list, here used as a literal accompaniment), and بر کنار uses بر for motion 'toward/onto' + کنار 'edge/bank' indicating their destination. The sequence gives manner/accompaniment and direction of their departure.

sentences.s332-s9: He bestowed on them robes, silver, and gold; he gave me one cup and a jewel for each one.
sentences.s332-s1: He ordered that a scribe go to him to bring paper, musk, and fragrance.
sentences.s332-s2: They wrote a covenant from the triumphant king of the realm, Keykhosrow the pious.
sentences.s332-s3: Concerning (or for) the spahbed Go‑Piltan — praised in manliness at every assembly.
sentences.s332-s4: That he be (may he be) foremost in the world — a sovereign, alert, a commander, and eloquent.
sentences.s332-s5: He, too, held the realm of Nimruz — the victorious commander of the glorious host.
sentences.s332-s6: They set it down under oath, under a golden seal, in accordance with the custom of Kay Khosrow the Just.
sentences.s332-s7: He gave him a charter and praised him, saying, 'May the land be prosperous for Rostam.'
sentences.s332-s8: Guests — who, with Zal and Sam, mounted on horseback — departed, with the zijs, to the bank.
